channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Trump and Rubio Insist Iran War Is Over, Even as Missiles Fly During Cease-Fire
item.description: The White House is turning to rhetorical leaps as President Trump tries to put the biggest political crisis of his presidency behind him.
item.dc:creator: David E. Sanger
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: Despite Secretary of State Marco Rubio’s declaration that the objectives of the war have been accomplished, they clearly have not.

item.title: President Trump’s Sudden U-Turn, and a $1 Billion Ballroom Proposal
item.description: Plus, a visit to the “Gates to Hell.”
item.dc:creator: Tracy Mumford, Will Jarvis, Margaret Kadifa, Jake Lucas, Ian Stewart and Ben Casselman
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Tom Brenner for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump said the sudden pause of a U.S. operation to escort ships through the Strait of Hormuz was based in part on “great progress” toward a peace agreement with Iran.

item.title: In Indiana Primary Elections, Most Trump-Backed Challengers Beat Incumbents
item.description: President Trump endorsed primary opponents against seven Republican lawmakers who opposed his redistricting push. At least five of the Trump-backed candidates have won their races.
item.dc:creator: Mitch Smith
item.media:credit: Cara Penquite/Associated Press
item.media:description: Voters at the polls in West Lafayette, Ind., on Tuesday.

item.title: Takeaways From Indiana Primary Elections 2026: Trump Gets Payback
item.description: The president helped unseat most of the state lawmakers he targeted after they rebuffed his call to draw new House maps to help Republicans.
item.dc:creator: Mitch Smith and Reid J. Epstein
item.media:credit: Maddie McGarvey for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Indiana Statehouse in Indianapolis.

item.title: Republicans Unveil Map Carving Up Tennessee’s Majority-Black House District
item.description: The General Assembly is expected to quickly approve the map, which slices up Memphis, a majority-Black city that makes up most of the state’s lone Democratic district.
item.dc:creator: Emily Cochrane
item.media:credit: Brad J. Vest for The New York Times
item.media:description: Tennessee legislators are seen on the House floor in Nashville on Wednesday morning, where they were expected to unveil a new House map that affects the city of Memphis.

item.title: Ted Turner, Creator of CNN and the 24-Hour News Cycle, Dies at 87
item.description: As one of the most important figures in media history, he oversaw a vast cable empire of news, sports and entertainment channels.
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Kandell
item.media:credit: Chip Somodevilla/Getty Images
item.media:description: Ted Turner was a visionary media mogul who dominated the cable television industry, and extended his reach into movies, major league baseball and even yachting.

item.title: Tofersen, a New Treatment for A.L.S., Reverses Symptoms for Some
item.description: The drug is for a small subset of patients. But evidence that breathing and strength can get better for some of them is remarkable for a paralyzing, usually fatal disease.
item.dc:creator: Pam Belluck
item.media:credit: Eva Marie Uzcategui for The New York Times

item.title: A Look Inside the Case That Enshrined Political Power for Billionaires
item.description: After Watergate, Congress tried to curtail the role of money in politics. But a pivotal Supreme Court case nipped it in the bud. Years later, new details are emerging on how wealthy Americans were conferred with a “right to spend” on elections.
item.dc:creator: Danny Hakim
item.category: Citizens United v Federal Election Commission (Supreme Court Decision)
item.media:credit: Keystone Press Agency/ZUMA Press Wire, via Reuters
item.media:description: A landmark Supreme Court case laid the groundwork for the tremendous amounts spent on today’s elections.

item.title: Leo XIV Is the First Pope From the U.S. He’s Making That an Asset.
item.description: A year into his papacy, Leo has shown a readiness to challenge Washington, defying any concerns about the election of an American pontiff.
item.dc:creator: Motoko Rich and Elizabeth Dias
item.media:credit: Gregorio Borgia/Associated Press
item.media:description: Pope Leo XIV arriving at St. Peter’s Square in the Vatican on Wednesday.

item.title: What Happened When Pope Leo XIV Had to Call Customer Service
item.description: Spoiler alert: There was no miracle.
item.dc:creator: Julie Bosman
item.media:credit: Alessandra Tarantino/Associated Press
item.media:description: Pope Leo XIV has a palace, the Swiss Guard and the popemobile. But he also has hassles, just like everyone else.

item.title: Weather Service Races to Rehire as Storm Season Arrives
item.description: After deep cuts last year, the agency is hiring hundreds. But fears linger that it isn’t equipped for imminent tornado and hurricane threats.
item.dc:creator: Scott Dance, Judson Jones and Amy Graff
item.media:credit: Julio Cortez/Associated Press
item.media:description: A member of the National Weather Service surveying a storm-damaged area in Mineral Wells, Texas, last week.

item.title: Howard Lutnick Faces Questions From Congress About Epstein Ties
item.description: The commerce secretary was in a closed-door session on Wednesday with the House Oversight Committee
item.dc:creator: Ana Swanson and Michael Gold
item.category: House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick on Capitol Hill in April. He is one of the highest-profile cabinet members to come under scrutiny in connection with Jeffrey Epstein.

item.title: Guy Goma’s Accidental BBC Interview Lives On After 20 Years
item.description: Guy Goma thought he was interviewing for an I.T. job when he found himself on air on the BBC. What came next was familiar to anyone who has been unprepared at work.
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Wolfe

item.title: 3 People Are Evacuated From Cruise Ship With Hantavirus Outbreak
item.description: Three people with possible symptoms of the disease were evacuated on Wednesday morning from the Dutch-flagged MV Hondius.
item.dc:creator: Claire Moses
item.media:credit: Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: MV Hondius off the port of Praia, the capital of Cape Verde, on Wednesday.

item.title: World Cup Dynamic Ticket Pricing Drives Argentine Soccer Fans To Extremes
item.description: Dynamic ticket pricing has made this year’s tournament the most expensive in history, pushing Argentine fans to extremes.
item.dc:creator: Tariq Panja, Emma Bubola, Lucía Cholakian Herrera and Sarah Pabst
item.media:description: River Plate soccer fans at Estadio Monumental stadium in Buenos Aires, at the biggest club game in Argentina against rival Boca Juniors last month.

item.title: In Romania, Living With Weapons of War Spilling Into NATO Territory
item.description: Four years of battle in Ukraine is taking a toll on neighboring Romanians. Even if the war has not crossed the border, drones have done so.
item.dc:creator: Lara Jakes and Andrada Lautaru
item.media:credit: Andreea Campeanu for The New York Times

item.title: Deadly Russian Strikes Rip Into Ukrainian Cities
item.description: At least 12 people were killed in Zaporizhzhia, one of the highest tolls from a single attack so far this year. The cities of Dnipro and Kramatorsk were also attacked.
item.dc:creator: Cassandra Vinograd
item.media:credit: Serhii Chalyi/Reuters
item.media:description: The site of a Russian airstrike in Zaporizhzhia, Ukraine, on Tuesday. The death toll in Zaporizhzhia was one of the highest from a single attack so far this year.

item.title: American Factories Lag in Adopting A.I. This Drugmaker Is an Exception.
item.description: A Bristol Myers Squibb plant that makes cancer drugs was the only manufacturer in the U.S. recognized for innovation by the World Economic Forum this year.
item.dc:creator: Farah Stockman
item.media:credit: Sophie Park for The New York Times
item.media:description: At the Bristol Myers Squibb facility in Devens, Mass., scientists use automated machines to help make cancer drugs.

item.title: Japan’s Takaichi Pitches Strength and Stability in Visits to Australia and Vietnam
item.description: Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi is attempting to raise Japan’s profile at a time when China’s clout is rising and doubts about the United States are spreading.
item.dc:creator: Javier C. Hernández
item.category: Far East, South and Southeast Asia and Pacific Areas
item.media:credit: Hollie Adams/Reuters
item.media:description: Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi of Japan arriving in Canberra, Australia, on Sunday.

item.title: Renewables Are Gaining on Fossil Fuels, IRENA Report Finds
item.description: Sources like wind and solar can now deliver continuous power, according to a new report. And, they’re often a bargain compared with fossil fuels.
item.dc:creator: Chico Harlan
item.media:credit: Ruth Fremson/The New York Times
item.media:description: Battery units for a solar farm facility in Antofagasta, Chile, last year. Cheaper and better batteries are making renewable power more practical.

item.title: John Roberts Believes in an America That Doesn’t Exist
item.description: Guess who benefits from a “colorblind Constitution.”
item.dc:creator: Jamelle Bouie
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times

item.title: You Can’t Be Born Here. You Can Only Die.
item.description: The existential pain of losing birth services.
item.dc:creator: Jessica Grose

item.title: The Cruise From Hell
item.description: We look into the hantavirus crisis onboard the Hondius.
item.dc:creator: Sam Sifton
item.media:credit: Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: The cruise ship MV Hondius near Cape Verde yesterday.

item.title: Late Night Cringes at Trump’s Chatter in Front of Children
item.description: “How are you talking about nuclear war in front of children?” Desi Lydic asked after the president discussed the Iran conflict in a room full of students on Tuesday.
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: Late Show with Stephen Colbert (TV Program)
Late Night with Seth Meyers (TV Program)
The Daily Show with Jon Stewart (TV Program)

item.title: How Did a State Auditor Become the ‘People’s Rockstar’?
item.description: Massachusetts voters approved a ballot measure in 2024 authorizing Diana DiZoglio, the state auditor, to closely scrutinize the State Legislature. Lawmakers are resisting.
item.dc:creator: Jenna Russell
item.media:credit: Sophie Park for The New York Times
item.media:description: Diana DiZoglio, a Democrat, has faced stiff resistance from lawmakers in her own party.

item.title: Reclaiming the Name of the Black Hero Who Inspired ‘Uncle Tom’s Cabin’
item.description: After 42 years of slavery, Josiah Henson escaped to Canada, where he wrote a memoir, founded a school and led others to freedom. But his home long bore the name “Uncle Tom,” to the offense of many.
item.dc:creator: Ian Austen and Ian Willms
item.media:credit: Ian Willms for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Henson House at the Josiah Henson Museum of African-Canadian History in Dresden, Canada.

item.title: Trial Begins for Man Accused of Running Secret Police Outpost for China
item.description: Lu Jianwang, a U.S. citizen, is accused of working on behalf of the Chinese government to target dissidents. A co-defendant has pleaded guilty.
item.dc:creator: Santul Nerkar
item.category: Ministry of Public Security of the People's Republic of China
item.media:credit: Andrew Seng for The New York Times
item.media:description: Lu Jianwang is accused of working on behalf of China out of an outpost in Manhattan.

item.title: Pollution Worsened in South Bronx After Congestion Tolls, Study Finds
item.description: Columbia University researchers said that sensors detected a 2 percent increase in toxic particles in the air. M.T.A. officials disputed the report.
item.dc:creator: Hilary Howard and Olivia Bensimon
item.media:credit: Jade Doskow for The New York Times
item.media:description: The South Bronx, which is divided by major highways and bridges, has some of the highest asthma rates in the United States.
